# Questions in Livestream 196

Live Stream #196: Remembering Kenneth A. Kitchen

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction |
|---:|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pGmOZ9jdOdQ?t=1039" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">17:19</a> | Do we know what ancient Egyptian music sounded like? | Only partly. He said we know instruments and some broad features, but not enough to reconstruct the full sound with confidence. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pGmOZ9jdOdQ?t=1168" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">19:28</a> | Can a demon say the name Yahweh? | He said yes; a demon can say the name, but cannot truthfully affirm "Jesus is Lord" without changing the meaning. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pGmOZ9jdOdQ?t=1457" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">24:17</a> | What do you make of older claims that Amorite speakers died out around 1600 BC? | He said that sort of discussion is dated and should not be treated as the last word on Amorite and related language history. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pGmOZ9jdOdQ?t=1623" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">27:03</a> | If Isaiah 53 predicts the Messiah, why is it written in past tense? | He answered in terms of Hebrew verbal aspect rather than simple western tense categories, so the form does not block prophecy. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pGmOZ9jdOdQ?t=1784" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">29:44</a> | Can you give examples of Hebrew perfect verbs being used this way? | He eventually pointed to Jonah 2, where he said the passage begins with perfect forms and later shifts to imperfect forms. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pGmOZ9jdOdQ?t=1933" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">32:13</a> | What do you think of the claim that Judges is likely a premonarchic text, and is Elie Assis reliable? | He had not yet read the book, but said the thesis sounded interesting and the BBS series generally has credibility. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pGmOZ9jdOdQ?t=2075" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">34:35</a> | Can you say more about death as separation, and is there further reading you recommend? | He restated death as separation but said he knew of little reading that treats the subject in quite the same way. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pGmOZ9jdOdQ?t=2311" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">38:31</a> | What about the Sadducees, since they did not believe in resurrection? | He said they still believed in body and spirit, but did not believe in resurrection. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pGmOZ9jdOdQ?t=2412" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">40:12</a> | Did Assyrians believe the dead go to a dark place where nothing happens? | He said that still is not ceasing to exist; they believed in body and spirit, even if nothing happens to the spirit. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pGmOZ9jdOdQ?t=2571" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">42:51</a> | Can Atenism be called monotheistic, is monolatry better, and is there any connection to Judaism? | He rejected monotheism, preferred a monolatrous or pantheistic framing, and said a connection is possible only in a broad collapsing-into-monotheism sense, not Freud's theory. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pGmOZ9jdOdQ?t=2698" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">44:58</a> | Were Egyptian gods thought literally to be the elements they were associated with? | He said Egyptian religion often allowed that kind of paradox: a god could control an element and also be identified with it. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pGmOZ9jdOdQ?t=2819" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">46:59</a> | What element was Set thought to control or be? | He identified Set as a storm deity and a god of power, especially uncontrolled power. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pGmOZ9jdOdQ?t=3138" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">52:18</a> | What is a good way to stay motivated when burned out on a project but unable to take a real break? | He recommended changing pace without abandoning the work, such as switching tasks, slowing down, walking, exercising, or briefly working somewhere else. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pGmOZ9jdOdQ?t=3500" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">58:20</a> | What is the lobster chili recipe? | He described a chili using lobster, guava or pineapple, orange bell pepper, and a little habanero, with no beans or other filler. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pGmOZ9jdOdQ?t=4067" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:07:47</a> | Have you ever done motorcycle repair? | He said no; he has never owned a motorcycle or done motorcycle repair. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pGmOZ9jdOdQ?t=4091" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:08:11</a> | What Old Testament scholarship issues would make an impactful dissertation proposal? | He suggested starting with a potential adviser, reading that scholar's work, building a relationship, and then narrowing a manageable research gap. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pGmOZ9jdOdQ?t=4474" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:14:34</a> | Are Atlantis or Thoth and the Emerald Tablets mentioned in Egyptian records? | He answered no, treating Atlantis as a Greek philosophical story from Plato and the Emerald Tablets as later myth rather than Egyptian records. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pGmOZ9jdOdQ?t=4638" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:17:18</a> | How can a fictional character be both LGBT+ and Christian without sending the wrong message? | He said such a character can be written, but the story should honestly portray the tension with historic Christian teaching rather than erase it. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pGmOZ9jdOdQ?t=5068" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:24:28</a> | Is it a stretch to see the chief baker's death and cupbearer's restoration in Genesis 40 as foreshadowing Jesus' body and blood? | He called that a very christocentric interpretation and said he prefers beginning with the Old Testament context before moving to christological readings. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pGmOZ9jdOdQ?t=6156" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:42:36</a> | If God does not change his mind, how do we understand him regretting the creation of humanity in Genesis 6:6-7? | He explained it through God's unchanging nature but changing relational position: God can draw near or away while his nature does not change. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pGmOZ9jdOdQ?t=6450" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:47:30</a> | Does Paul say in Acts 17 that God is not far away from any of us? | He said Acts 17 is about relationship and separation from God, not a denial of divine transcendence. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pGmOZ9jdOdQ?t=6744" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:52:24</a> | Is the pharaoh who died in Exodus 2:23 Seti I? | He said the text is ambiguous; Seti I, Horemheb, or even Ramesses I are possible depending on the assumed chronology. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pGmOZ9jdOdQ?t=6925" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:55:25</a> | What is the saltiest thing you remember Kenneth Kitchen ranting about, if appropriate to share? | He recalled Kitchen sharply rejecting a documentary-hypothesis presentation at Tyndale House and using phrases like "I'm not convinced" and "rubbish." |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pGmOZ9jdOdQ?t=7598" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:06:38</a> | Why did God rest on the seventh day in Genesis 2:2? | He said God did not rest from tiredness, but to establish Sabbath precedent and relationship apart from work. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pGmOZ9jdOdQ?t=7978" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:12:58</a> | Why are curse or swear words considered unchristian? | He distinguished curses, profanity, and unwholesome speech, arguing that Scripture targets destructive speech like slander more directly than culturally defined profanity. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pGmOZ9jdOdQ?t=9283" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:34:43</a> | If the serpent is a spirit creature, why is it cursed to creep on its belly and eat dust like a snake? | He said the language is analogical, shaming a high cherub by depicting a fall from heavenly status to earthbound humiliation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pGmOZ9jdOdQ?t=9568" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:39:28</a> | Is "serpent" a kind of retroactive title meant to shame the cherub? | He said it is definitely shaming and mocking language, though he preferred calling it analogical rather than retroactive. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pGmOZ9jdOdQ?t=9662" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:41:02</a> | Weren't snakes seen in high regard because they represented rebirth or something similar? | He said some serpent imagery, such as seraphim, could be honorable and protective, but the garden serpent image is about shame and demotion. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pGmOZ9jdOdQ?t=9987" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:46:27</a> | Did Egypt have a flat-earth cosmology? | He said no; Egyptian cosmology is better pictured as a cyclical solar path or "treadmill" model than as flat earth. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pGmOZ9jdOdQ?t=10583" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:56:23</a> | Did ancient Near Eastern people associate the planets with the seven archangels? | He said planets and stars could be associated with spiritual beings, but not with seven archangels in the way asked, partly because only five planets were known. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pGmOZ9jdOdQ?t=10899" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:01:39</a> | Why is David Hume taken seriously in scholarship? | He said Hume was foundational for British empiricism and critiques of theism, but modern scholarship has largely moved beyond him. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pGmOZ9jdOdQ?t=11091" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:04:51</a> | Do you have a guitarist who has been influential for you? | He said his guitar use is mostly classical music and composition rather than modern guitar influence. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pGmOZ9jdOdQ?t=11358" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:09:18</a> | Do you remember news reports about Chernobyl, and where did you learn the danger reached outside the USSR? | He said he was about 15 or 16 and heard about it on the news, noting the West learned after radioisotopes were detected outside the USSR. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pGmOZ9jdOdQ?t=12218" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:23:38</a> | Have you read Amos Yong's book on disability and the church? | He had not read it and could not recommend it, but said disability deserves more attention in the church. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pGmOZ9jdOdQ?t=12426" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:27:06</a> | What is your favorite genre of music? | He said his tastes are broad, including soundtracks, classical music, heavy metal, trance, EDM, and some vampire-club-associated bands. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pGmOZ9jdOdQ?t=12703" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:31:43</a> | Is the underdetermination of data a hard problem for classical foundationalism, as presuppositionalists argue? | He said no and rejected the presuppositional claim that one cannot know anything without knowing God. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pGmOZ9jdOdQ?t=13044" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:37:24</a> | Why do many Modern Hebrew speakers and Messianic Jews pronounce Yahweh as Yahovah? | He attributed it to vowel pointing intended to avoid pronouncing the divine name directly. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pGmOZ9jdOdQ?t=13520" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:45:20</a> | Can you talk about tattooing in ancient Egypt? | He said tattoos are found on mummies, especially women and some priests, often with symmetrical placement, but written evidence is limited. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pGmOZ9jdOdQ?t=13911" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:51:51</a> | How would you recommend researching and approaching a new topic? | He recommended starting from credible sources, reading their bibliographies, and recursively following reliable bibliography trails. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pGmOZ9jdOdQ?t=14115" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:55:15</a> | Were the disciples young men when they were with Jesus? | He said they were generally young men, often teenagers or early twenties, but not children; some were already married. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pGmOZ9jdOdQ?t=14427" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:00:27</a> | What was the marriage age for women in ancient Israel and the ancient Near East? | He said the normal range was roughly mid-to-late teens, often about 15 to 19, shaped by agrarian survival pressures. |
